A flight number denotes a scheduled service that recurs over time; each date on which that service runs is a distinct operated flight, with its own aircraft, crew and outcome.
Each flight is operated by exactly one airline and uses exactly one aircraft.
Each flight has a departure airport and an arrival airport; these are distinct operational roles, because gates, timings and handling differ between them.
An aircraft belongs to a model, and many aircraft can share the same model.

A flight's operational status is one value from a controlled set of states (for example on-time, delayed, cancelled, boarding, departed, arrived); the status changes over time, and each change is recorded with its date and time.
Each airport is located in a city.
Baggage for a flight is handled on a baggage belt, and each baggage belt belongs to an airport.
A person can travel on many flights over time, and each flight carries many people; on a given flight, each seat is occupied by at most one traveller.
Crew members are assigned to flights: a flight has several crew members and a crew member works on many flights over time. Each crew member has a role.
